abstract:
This study evaluates the quality of advocacy in youth justice proceedings to identify the essential elements of effective legal representation. Conducted on behalf of the Bar Standards Board, the research sought to determine if regulatory interventions are necessary to elevate professional standards in this specialized field. The findings are based on a comprehensive methodology that included a survey of 215 legal advocates, interviews with 96 key stakeholders—ranging from young defendants and magistrates to Youth Offending Team workers—and detailed observations across four Youth Courts and five Crown Courts in England and Wales.

The report concludes that the performance of advocates cannot be assessed in a vacuum; rather, it is deeply intertwined with the broader legal, institutional, and cultural environments of the justice system. To address identified shortcomings, the study offers a series of recommendations aimed at fostering more impactful advocacy. These proposals focus on reforming the structural frameworks of youth proceedings, empowering court-based facilitators to better support legal representatives, and enhancing specialized training and lifelong learning opportunities for advocates.
